0.title: What Is Malloy
0.excerpt: Malloy is a modern open source language for describing data relationships and transformations. It is an analytical language that runs on SQL databases. It provides the ability to define a semantic data model and query it. Malloy currently works with SQL databases BigQuery, Postgr

1.title: Cohort Analysis
1.excerpt: It is often useful to see how groups of people behave over time. The most simple example is in retail. Of the people that first signed up for accouunts how many made an order and how many ordered in subsequent months.

2.title: Transforming Data
2.excerpt: Malloy can be used to for data transformation. Files with the extension .malloysql are interpreted as a combination of both Malloy and SQL. Each language is blocked by a preceeding `>>>`. Each block can do anything that is appropriate in the language. In Malloy, for

3.title: Working with Nested Data
3.excerpt: Data often comes in a nested structure, where information is organized hierarchically. BigQuery and DuckDB have built-in support for reading tables with nested data and extracting information from these nested structures.

4.title: Sessionized Data - Map/Reduce
4.excerpt: Flight event data contains _dep_time_, _carrier_, _origin_, _destination_ and _tail_num_ (the plane that made the flight). The query below takes the flight event data and maps it into sessions of _flight_date_, _carrier_, and _tail_num_. For each session, a nested list of _flight

5.title: Computing Moving Averages
5.excerpt: Malloy can compute moving averages on resultsets.

6.title: Automatically Binning Data
6.excerpt: By examining the range of values over a dataset, we can compute the appropriate histogram bin size, while capturing the data at the same time. We can then pipe the output to another query to display a histogram.

7.title: Bucketing with 'Other'
7.excerpt: Often you want to limit the number of group-by values in a table, and bucket everything else into an 'other' category.

8.title: Foreign Sums
8.excerpt: Malloy allows you to compute sums and averages correctly based on your join tree. Fan-outs based on join relationships will never impact the correctness of these aggregations. This example has `flights`, joining to `aircraft`, joining to `aircraft_model``.

9.title: Malloy Dimensional Indexes
9.excerpt: Malloy has a special query operator that builds _Dimensional Search Indexes for sources. A Dimensional Search Index is a table with 4 columns. Dimensional indexes are useful for a variety of things including filtering suggestions and LLMs.

10.title: Nested Subtotals
10.excerpt: Nested subtotals are quite painful to do in SQL, requiring either self-joins, window functions, or `GROUP BY ROLLUP` queries. Unfortunately for analysts, it's also a very common type of analysis requested by business owners; for example, determing which segments of a population d

11.title: Measuring Change over Time by Comparing Timeframes
11.excerpt: There are a couple of different ways to go about this in Malloy.

12.title: Percent of Total
12.excerpt: Malloy provides a way to compute _percent of total_ through level of detail (ungrouped aggregates) functions. The functions `all()` and `exclude()` escape grouping in aggregate calculations. These functions are different than window functions as they operate inline with the query

13.title: Directly Querying a JSON API
13.excerpt: DuckDB can be used to query a JSON API endpoint, and Malloy makes it simple to transform the resulting data. The example below reads exchange rate data from the US Treasury and shows the data by currency over time.

14.title: Publish Your Models
14.excerpt: Publisher serves Malloy models through APIs—for tools, applications, and AI agents.

15.title: Publisher Connections
15.excerpt: Configure database connections for Malloy Publisher deployments. For an overview of supported databases, see [Database Support](../../setup/database_support.malloynb).

16.title: REST API
16.excerpt: Build custom applications that query your Malloy models via HTTP.

17.title: Explorer: Visual Query Builder
17.excerpt: Explorer is a no-code, visual query builder for Malloy. Use it yourself in VS Code during development, or serve it to your entire team via Publisher.

18.title: MCP for AI Agents
18.excerpt: Connect AI assistants to your Malloy semantic models using the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Publisher includes a built-in MCP server that exposes your models to AI agents.

19.title: Publisher SDK: Embed Analytics in React
19.excerpt: Build custom data applications with the Publisher SDK. Embed live, governed analytics from your Malloy models directly into React apps.

20.title: Transform & Materialize
20.excerpt: This guide covers how to run materializations with the Malloy CLI and MalloySQL—creating tables, views, and exports from your Malloy queries.

21.title: Malloy Quickstart
21.excerpt: This guide introduces the basics of querying data and building a semantic model with the Malloy language. By the end of this tutorial, you will understand how to use Malloy to run queries, build re-usable data models, and do analysis on your data that is nearly impossible in SQL.

22.title: Sql Experts3
22.excerpt: A query block alwyas follows a `->` operator and is used to transform data. Components of a query block include `group_by:`, `agggregate:`, `select:`, `order_by:`, `limit:`, `calculate:`, `where:`, `having:` and `index:`. The query blocks can be chained in a pipeline, for example

23.title: More Guidance for SQL Users
23.excerpt: This guide expands on information shared in [Malloy for SQL Folks](https://docs.malloydata.dev/documentation/user_guides/sql_experts1).

24.title: Malloy for dbt Users
24.excerpt: This guide maps common dbt patterns to their Malloy equivalents, using the flights dataset.

25.title: Malloy for SQL Folks
25.excerpt: This document will to move you quickly into the Malloy language by showing how different queries translate SQL into Malloy.

26.title: Querying a Semantic Data Model
26.excerpt: A semantic data model contains prebuilt calculations and relationships for a set of data. This article will show you the basics of querying a semantic data model in [Malloy](https://www.malloydata.dev).

27.title: Analyzing with Notebooks
27.excerpt: Malloy Notebooks (`.malloynb` files) combine queries, results, and documentation in a single interactive document. They're the best way to explore data, build analyses, and share findings.

28.title: Advanced Modeling
28.excerpt: This guide covers advanced Malloy features for building sophisticated semantic models. It assumes you've completed [Building a Semantic Model](quickstart_modeling.malloynb) and are familiar with sources, dimensions, measures, views, and basic joins.

29.title: Building a Semantic Model
29.excerpt: A semantic model is the interface to your data—it defines what a "customer" is, how "revenue" is calculated, which tables relate to each other. Instead of scattering this logic across SQL queries, you define it once and reuse it everywhere.

30.title: Troubleshooting
30.excerpt: Jump to the section that matches your symptom:

31.title: MalloySQL
31.excerpt: MalloySQL enables mixing dialect-specific SQL (including DDL) with Malloy. For example, MalloySQL can be used to create a new table in BigQuery, based on a model of existing data.

32.title: Using Malloy in Jupyter Notebooks
32.excerpt: Malloy provides built-in IPython magic commands that allow you to define Malloy models and run Malloy queries inside of Jupyter notebooks. [Click here](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1eo1fe3878DfUvo1RcyhFUKcFhjdaSlzp?usp=sharing) to see a simple example of this in Colab.

33.title: The Malloy Python Package
33.excerpt: Malloy provides a Python package that allows you to load Malloy model files, compile Malloy queries, and run Malloy statements on your database from within your Python application or script. The package is open source, MIT-licensed, and hosted in the [malloy-py Github repo](https

34.title: Composite "Cube" Sources
34.excerpt: Composite sources in Malloy are virtualized sources which are backed by multiple other tables or sources. This is useful for handling cubed / rolled up data.

35.title: Joins
35.excerpt: Allow you to use INNER, RIGHT and FULL joins.

36.title: Parameters Experiment
36.excerpt: The parameters experiment enables the ability to add parameters to source definitions.

37.title: Build all .malloy files in the current directory (recursive)
37.excerpt: Malloy sources backed by queries can be "persisted" — their results saved as database tables. When queries run against a persistent source, the runtime reads from the pre-built table instead of recomputing the query.

38.title: Sql Expressions
38.excerpt: Malloy allows you to call native database functions using `!type`. For example if you wanted to call the duckdb function bit_length that returns a number, in Malloy you couild write `bitlength!number("this string)`.

39.title: Experimental Features
39.excerpt: Before releasing language features, we like to get a feel for how they work when used in programs. We often find bugs or language problems. We like when people use these features with the understanding that they might change in their final form or never be released.

40.title: Access Modifiers Experiment
40.excerpt: When developing Malloy models, it is fairly common to have a table which has more fields than you need, or fields which are useful for intermediary computation but should not be part of the external interface of the modeled source. To enable better curation of sources, we've adde

41.title: Givens — Model-Level Parameters
41.excerpt: **Givens** are named values supplied to a Malloy model at run time. A model declares the values it expects with a top-level `given:` block; references to those values inside the model use a `$NAME` sigil; the host application supplies values when constructing a `Runtime` or runni

42.title: Virtual Sources
42.excerpt: Malloy's `type:` declaration and `::` type operator introduce two related capabilities:

44.title: CLI Setup
44.excerpt: The [Malloy CLI](https://github.com/malloydata/malloy-cli) is a command-line interface for running `.malloysql` and `.malloy` files. Use it to automate transformations, build pipelines, or compile Malloy queries to SQL.

45.title: Configuration: `malloy-config.json`
45.excerpt: Both the [VS Code Extension](extension.malloynb) and the [Malloy CLI](cli.malloynb) use a `malloy-config.json` file to configure database connections. This page documents the shared file format, connection types, and environment variable options.

46.title: VS Code Extension
46.excerpt: The [Malloy VS Code Extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=malloydata.malloy-vscode) is the primary authoring environment for Malloy models. It includes schema browsing, query execution, language auto-complete, documentation, and result visualizations.

47.title: Database Support
47.excerpt: Malloy connects to a variety of databases. This page provides an overview of supported databases and links to platform-specific setup guides.

48.title: Malloy Filter Expressions
48.excerpt: Malloy includes specialized sub-languages for filtering [string](#string-filter-expressions), [number](#numeric-filter-expressions), [date](#temporal-filter-expressions), [timestamp](#temporal-filter-expressions) or [boolean](#boolean-filter-expressions) data. There is a speciali

49.title: Connections
49.excerpt: In Malloy, named connection objects are used to interact with a database, including fetching schemas for SQL queries and tables, as well as actually running queries.

50.title: Expressions Involving NULL
50.excerpt: Malloy and SQL use a "NULL tainting" approoach to null values in computation. Many computation which include a `null` produce a `null` result.

51.title: Functions
51.excerpt: Malloy has a standard set of functions available at all times. These include scalar functions (e.g. `concat`), aggregate functions (e.g. `stddev`), and analytic (or "window") functions (e.g. `lag`).

52.title: 4.0 Messages FAQ
52.excerpt: There are a few places where Malloy 4.0 will require existing code to change. In the transitional period, both the 4.0 and Pre-4.0 syntax is accepted, and a warning will be generated for Pre-4.0 constructs, to give users guidance in changing their code. Once 4.0 fully releases, t

53.title: Timezones
53.excerpt: > NOTE: While full timezone support exists in the calculation component of

54.title: Timestamp and Date Operations
54.excerpt: For truncation to a time unit (timeframe), use the period (`.`) operator followed by the desired timeframe.

55.title: Data Types In Malloy
55.excerpt: Because Malloy writes SQL expressions, when queries are actually executed, all data will be in types understood by the SQL engine which is running the query.

56.title: Views
56.excerpt: Malloy separates queries into a *source* and a *view* on that source. Views can be defined in a source or used directly in queries. Views that are named as part of a [source extension](./source.malloynb#source-extensions) can be reused, nested, and refined.

57.title: SQL Blocks
57.excerpt: Sometimes it is useful to add SQL statements into a Malloy file. You can do so by using the `sql:` keyword.

58.title: Filters
58.excerpt: Filtering which data is used in a query is an incredibly important aspect of data analysis. Malloy makes it easy to target specific parts of a query to apply individual filters.

59.title: Ordering and Limiting
59.excerpt: Often when querying data the amount of data returned to look at is much smaller than the full result set, so the ordering of the data makes a big difference in what you actually see. To make things easier, Malloy has some smart defaults in the way it presents data. For the most p

60.title: Aggregates
60.excerpt: Malloy supports the standard aggregate functions `count`, `sum`, `avg`, `min`, and `max`. When these are used in a field's definition, they make that field a [measure](fields.malloynb#measures).

61.title: Evaluation Spaces
61.excerpt: All expressions in Malloy have an "evaluation space" which can be one of four values: _literal_, _constant_, _input_, and _output_.

62.title: Expressions
62.excerpt: Expressions in Malloy are much like expressions in any other language; they can have variables and operators and function calls in

63.title: Sources
63.excerpt: Sources, and in particular, [extended sources](#source-extensions), are Malloy's primary unit of reusability for defining computations, join relationships, and queries.

64.title: Functions Changes in v0.0.39
64.excerpt: In Malloy version 0.0.39, treatment of functions changed dramatically.

65.title: Malloy time range expressions
65.excerpt: There are two forms of range expressions

66.title: Queries
66.excerpt: The basic syntax for a query in Malloy consists of a [source](./source.malloynb) and a [view](./views.malloynb) consisting of one or more _stages_ separated by `->`. The shape of the data defined in the original source is transformed by each stage.

67.title: Imports
67.excerpt: In order to reuse or extend a source from another file, you can include all the

68.title: Nested Views
68.excerpt: Nested views are views included in other views. When a nested view is used in a query, it generates an _aggregating subquery_, which produces a subtable per row in the view in which it is embedded. The term _nested query_ is often used to refer to a query which uses a nested view

69.title: Presto / Trino Dialect
69.excerpt: Malloy supports Presto and Trino databases with two dialects which are closely related.

72.title: PostgreSQL
72.excerpt: The Malloy PostgreSQL connection uses JSON intermediate results, which limits integer precision to JavaScript's safe integer range (±9,007,199,254,740,991). Integer values larger than this may lose precision in query results.

73.title: MSSQL via DuckDB
73.excerpt: Malloy can query Microsoft SQL Server through DuckDB's

76.title: Databricks
76.excerpt: Databricks uses the [Databricks SQL Connector](https://docs.databricks.com/en/dev-tools/nodejs-sql-driver.html) to connect to Databricks SQL warehouses and clusters.

77.title: SQL Sources
77.excerpt: _SQL sources, introduced in Malloy version 0.56, replace the previous method of including SQL queries in a Malloy model, [SQL blocks](./sql_blocks.malloynb)._

78.title: Models
78.excerpt: Malloy recognizes modeling as a key aspect of data analytics and provides tools that allow for modularity and reusability of definitions. Whereas in SQL, queries generally define all metrics inline, requiring useful snippets to be saved and managed separately, in Malloy,

79.title: Joins
79.excerpt: Joins in Malloy differ from SQL joins. When two sources are joined,

81.title: HyperLogLog Algorithm Overview
81.excerpt: The HyperLogLog algorithm is a probabilistic cardinality estimator used to approximate the number

82.title: Ungrouped Aggregates
82.excerpt: In a query which is grouped by multiple dimensions, it is often useful to be able to perform an aggregate calculation on sub-groups.

83.title: Apply (?) Operator
83.excerpt: The apply operator takes one expression and applies it to another.

84.title: Fields
84.excerpt: Fields constitute all kinds of data in Malloy. They

85.title: Calculations and Window Functions
85.excerpt: Window functions in Malloy are expressed in _calculation_ fields, using the `calculate: ` keyword. Calculation fields operate on the results of a aggregation or selection, while still operating within the same query stage. Logically these calculation operations occur "after" the

86.title: Annotation
86.excerpt: Annotations are text strings attached to objects when a Malloy file compiles. They carry metadata — display hints for the renderer, compiler flags, documentation strings, application-specific configuration. The compiler stores annotations verbatim and hands them back to whichever

87.title: Malloy's Features
87.excerpt: SQL is a powerful language that can express any possible query, but it has its downsides: everything is expressible, but nothing is reusable; simple ideas are complex to express; the language is verbose and lacks smart defaults. Malloy is immediately understandable by SQL users,

88.title: The Tao of Malloy
88.excerpt: *Tao is the natural order of the universe whose character one's human intuition must discern in order to realize the potential for individual wisdom. This intuitive knowing of "life" cannot be grasped as a concept; it is known through actual living experience of one's everyday be

89.title: Error Dictionary
89.excerpt: The types of each `pick` (and the `else` if it is present), in a pick statement must match.

90.title: Segment Maps
90.excerpt: The plugin currently supports US maps. Segment maps take as input 4 columns: start latitude, start longitude, end latitude, and end longitude of the segment.

91.title: Lists
91.excerpt: Tables with one or two elements can be rendered as lists. Lists improve information density.

92.title: bar_chart
92.excerpt: When Malloy runs a query, it returns two things. The *results* of the query and *metadata* about the results. The metadata are the schema for the results, including type information. Malloy also provides a mechanism to tag things in the source code and return tags with this meta

93.title: Rendering Numbers
93.excerpt: Malloy's render provides a variety of ways to render numeric values. Tagging a number will change how the number is displayed in the result set but the underlying value doesn't change.

94.title: Dashboards
94.excerpt: The `# dashboard` tag renders a query result as a dashboard layout. Dimensions appear at the top, while aggregates and nested views float within the dashboard.

95.title: Bar Charts
95.excerpt: Malloy can create simple bar charts and bar charts with series breakdowns, either stacked or grouped.

97.title: Big Value Cards
97.excerpt: The `# big_value` tag renders aggregate values as prominent metric cards, ideal for KPIs and summary statistics. Big values support number formatting, comparison indicators, sparklines, and documentation tooltips.

98.title: Model-Level Rendering Options
98.excerpt: Malloy supports model-level annotations (using `##`) that configure rendering defaults across an entire model. These are set at the top of a file, outside any source or query.

99.title: Links
99.excerpt: Results can be annotated so they are rendered with links to external URLs.

100.title: Scatter Charts
100.excerpt: Scatter charts compare two numeric values.

101.title: Transposed Tables
101.excerpt: For comparison, it is often interesting to turn a table on its side. The `# transpose` tag on a query does just that.

102.title: Shape Maps
102.excerpt: The plugin currently supports US maps and state names.

103.title: Pivoting Results
103.excerpt: Malloy's renderer has a flexible and powerful way of pivoting data.

104.title: Tables
104.excerpt: Tables are the default rendering for query results. While tables work without any tags, there are several options for controlling column display, table width, and nested data.

106.title: About the Malloy CLI
106.excerpt: The [Malloy CLI](https://github.com/malloydata/malloy-cli) is a command-line interface for running `.malloysql` and `.malloy` files and building persistent tables. It can be used to automate transformations, build simple pipelines, or even integrate compiled Malloy SQL into other
